topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp morning air did little to settle the restless energy buzzing beneath Elias's skin. He paced the length of his study, his fingers drumming a frantic rhythm on the mahogany desk. Every time he glanced at the manuscript, a cold knot tightened in his stomach. It was impossible. Julian Vance, the titan of speculative fiction, had built his reputation on originality. Yet, the core narrative structure of Elias’s unpublished novella, "The Obsidian Labyrinth," was mirrored, almost perfectly, in Vance's latest bestseller. Elias leaned back, running a hand through his already disheveled hair. His breath hitched slightly as he reread the passage—a passage he had poured months of sleepless nights into crafting. A tremor ran through his hand as he snatched up his phone, the screen illuminating a stark, unwelcome truth.

[story 2]

A prickling sensation crawled up Maya’s neck, a tight wire vibrating with suppressed tension. She kept smoothing the edges of the printed pages, her movements precise, almost mechanical. The silence of her apartment amplified the frantic thumping in her chest. She’d spent the last hour cross-referencing stylistic tics, searching for the tell-tale fingerprints of imitation. When the undeniable similarity struck her—a specific, convoluted metaphor she’d used only once—a sharp intake of breath escaped her. Her eyes scanned the footnotes, the margins, searching for a slip-up, a misplaced word that would prove her theory. A sudden, almost painful clarity washed over her, leaving her feeling both intensely focused and profoundly unsettled.

[story 3]

The sunlight streaming through the blinds felt abrasive against Clara’s eyelids, making her squint. She sat bolt upright in bed, the sheets tangling around her legs. Her heart hammered against her ribs, a frantic drum solo against the quiet room. She had spent the entire afternoon reviewing the comparison documents, the evidence stacking up like incriminating stones. Every time she looked at the copied sentences, a fierce, defensive heat rose in her cheeks. "This isn't right," she whispered to the empty room, her voice tight with disbelief. She pushed herself up, the floorboards protesting softly under her weight, needing to move, needing to confront the sheer audacity of the deception staring back at her.

Every time he glanced at the manuscript, a cold knot tightened in his stomach. It was impossible. Julian Vance, the titan of speculative fiction, had built his reputation on originality. Yet, the core narrative structure of Elias’s unpublished novella, "The Obsidian Labyrinth," was mirrored, almost perfectly, in Vance's latest bestseller. Elias leaned back, running a hand through his already disheveled hair. His breath hitched slightly as he reread the passage—a passage he had poured months of sleepless nights into crafting. A tremor ran through his hand as he snatched up his phone, the screen illuminating a stark, unwelcome truth.

A prickling sensation crawled up Maya’s neck, a tight wire vibrating with suppressed tension. She kept smoothing the edges of the printed pages, her movements precise, almost mechanical. The silence of her apartment amplified the frantic thumping in her chest. She’d spent the last hour cross-referencing stylistic tics, searching for the tell-tale fingerprints of imitation. When the undeniable similarity struck her—a specific, convoluted metaphor she’d used only once—a sharp intake of breath escaped her. Her eyes scanned the footnotes, the margins, searching for a slip-up, a misplaced word that would prove her theory. A sudden, almost painful clarity washed over her, leaving her feeling both intensely focused and profoundly unsettled.

The sunlight streaming through the blinds felt abrasive against Clara’s eyelids, making her squint. She sat bolt upright in bed, the sheets tangling around her legs. Her heart hammered against her ribs, a frantic drum solo against the quiet room. She had spent the entire afternoon reviewing the comparison documents, the evidence stacking up like incriminating stones. Every time she looked at the copied sentences, a fierce, defensive heat rose in her cheeks. "This isn't right," she whispered to the empty room, her voice tight with disbelief. She pushed herself up, the floorboards protesting softly under her weight, needing to move, needing to confront the sheer audacity of the deception staring back at her.
